threats.title: AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal Missing isHooked Market Validation Allows Arbitrary Credential State Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal does not validate that msg.sender is a registered hooked market. Unlike onDeposit and onTransfer (which check _hookedMarkets[msg.sender].isHooked), and unlike FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal (which has the check), this function can be called by any external contract. This triggers _tryValidateAccess, which writes to _lenderStatus storage and makes stateful external calls to role providers via _tryValidateCredential. An attacker can: (1) force-expire a lender's credential by calling when their credential has lapsed, causing unsetCredential to be written to storage; (2) trigger stateful validateCredential calls on providers via crafted hooksData, consuming one-time-use tokens or causing other side effects without the lender's consent; (3) grief lenders by manipulating their credential state before time-sensitive deposit or withdrawal operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct access control bypass. The FixedTermLoanHooks version has the isHooked check but AccessControlHooks does not, indicating the check was likely intended but omitted. The function modifies critical storage (_lenderStatus) and makes external stateful calls, making the impact high.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onQueueWithdrawal has no isHooked check on msg.sender; directly calls _tryValidateAccess which writes _lenderStatus to storage. Compare with FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal which checks _hookedMarkets[msg.sender].isHooked at line entry.

preconditions: AccessControlHooks is deployed and has at least one registered role provider
Target lender has an existing _lenderStatus entry (e.g., expired or active credential)
Attacker can call onQueueWithdrawal from any address (no access restriction)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing market validation check that exists in peer functions and sibling contract
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any address can call onQueueWithdrawal(victim, 0, 0, fakeState, maliciousHooksData) to trigger _tryValidateAccess → _tryValidateAccessInner → _writeLenderStatus, writing lender credential state to storage
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No _hookedMarkets[msg.sender].isHooked check (present in onDeposit, onTransfer, and FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal)

analysis_questions: Can an attacker force-unset an active lender's credential by calling from an unregistered address?
Can the crafted hooksData trigger validateCredential on a provider with irreversible side effects?
Does isKnownLenderOnMarket[lender][attacker_address] always return false, guaranteeing _tryValidateAccess is always invoked?
Can this be chained with other hooks to prevent a lender from depositing or withdrawing?
validation_requirements: Deploy AccessControlHooks and call onQueueWithdrawal from a non-market address
Verify _lenderStatus is modified in storage after the call
Test with hooksData containing a real provider address to verify stateful call occurs
Compare behavior with FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal to confirm the missing check

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys contract that calls AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal(victimLender, 0, 0, fakeMarketState, providerAddress+maliciousData). Since msg.sender is not a hooked market, isKnownLenderOnMarket is false, forcing _tryValidateAccess. If victim has expired credential, it gets unset from storage. If hooksData targets a stateful provider, validateCredential is called consuming the victim's credential token.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Lenders can be denied access to deposits or withdrawals by having credentials force-expired; one-time-use credentials can be burned without consent
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lenders using AccessControlHooks on any market managed by this hooks instance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lender must re-authenticate with a provider, but one-time credentials may be permanently consumed

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying that onQueueWithdrawal rejects calls from non-hooked-market addresses

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time a target lender has a credential
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain event emission would show AccountAccessRevoked from unexpected context

threats.title: Gradual APR Step-Down Bypasses Temporary Reserve Ratio Protection on Fixed-Term Markets
threats.hypothesis_description: The onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips mechanism allows a borrower to reduce APR by up to 25% without any increase in the temporary reserve ratio (since relativeDiff <= 2500 returns originalReserveRatioBips). After the 2-week expiry, the new lower APR becomes the baseline for the next reduction. By repeating this pattern every 2 weeks, a borrower can reduce APR from 10000 to ~3164 bips (~68% reduction) in 8 weeks without ever triggering an elevated reserve ratio above the original. For fixed-term markets where lenders cannot withdraw, this traps them at dramatically reduced yields with no recourse.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _calculateTemporaryReserveRatioBips function explicitly allows up to 25% relative reductions penalty-free. Combined with the step-down pattern and the fixed-term lockup, this creates a meaningful economic exploit where borrowers reduce their cost of capital while lenders are locked in.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (relativeDiff <= 2500) { temporaryReserveRatioBips = uint16(originalReserveRatioBips); } — allows 25% reduction with NO penalty above the original reserve ratio. After expiry, the onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips recalculates from the NEW baseline via (intermediateState.annualInterestBips, intermediateState.reserveRatioBips) when tmp.expiry == 0.

preconditions: Borrower controls APR changes via market contract
Fixed-term market with locked lender funds
2 weeks between each step to allow temporary ratio to expire

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: 25% threshold allows penalty-free reduction; baseline resets after expiry
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After canExpire triggers and temporary is deleted, intermediateState.annualInterestBips reflects the REDUCED APR, which becomes the new originalAnnualInterestBips for subsequent reductions
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No cumulative tracking of total APR reduction from original market creation APR; no minimum APR floor relative to creation APR

analysis_questions: What is the maximum APR reduction achievable over the maximum fixed term (365 days) using 25% steps?
Does the temporary reserve ratio state correctly reset the baseline after expiry?
Could this be combined with setFixedTermEndTime(0) to release lenders after APR is minimized?
validation_requirements: Simulate a series of 25% APR reductions with 2-week intervals
Verify the baseline resets after each expiry
Calculate the total APR reduction over a 365-day fixed term

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Borrower creates FixedTermLoanHooks market with 365-day term and 10000 bips APR. Lenders deposit expecting high yield. Every 2 weeks borrower reduces APR by 25%: 10000→7500→5625→4219→3164→2373→1780→1335→1001→751→563→422→317→238→178→134→100. Over ~32 weeks, APR drops from 100% to ~1% without penalty reserve ratio elevation. Lenders remain locked for up to 365 days at dramatically reduced yields.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Lenders locked at dramatically reduced yields relative to what they deposited for; potential >90% reduction in expected yield
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lenders on the fixed-term market

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for cumulative multi-step APR reductions over time

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Fully visible on-chain but lenders cannot react if locked in fixed term

threats.title: Shared _lenderStatus Across Multiple Markets Enables Cross-Market Credential Leakage
threats.hypothesis_description: Both AccessControlHooks and FixedTermLoanHooks store lender credentials in a single _lenderStatus mapping shared across all markets managed by the same hooks instance. A credential granted for one market (e.g., a permissive low-risk market) automatically grants access to all other markets using the same hooks. While isKnownLenderOnMarket is correctly per-market, the _lenderStatus credential check happens first in _tryValidateAccessInner and succeeds before market-specific checks. This means a lender credentialed for Market A can deposit in Market B without separate approval for Market B.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _lenderStatus mapping is keyed only by lender address, not by (lender, market). The _tryValidateAccessInner function checks _lenderStatus first. If the credential is valid, it returns (true, false) immediately, bypassing any market-specific credential check. This is a design-level access control flaw.
deployment_assumptions: Same hooks instance manages multiple markets with different intended access policies
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence of per-market hooks instances. If borrower deploys one hooks contract managing multiple markets, this leakage applies.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _lenderStatus[accountAddress] is read without any market-context key. If lastProvider is still supported and credential not expired, returns (true, false) immediately — no per-market check.

preconditions: Single hooks instance manages two or more markets
Markets have different intended access policies
A lender is approved via a role provider for Market A
The same lender attempts to deposit on Market B using the same hooks instance

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: _lenderStatus mapping keyed only by address, not by (address, market); credential check does not consider which market the request is for
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Credential from Market A is accepted for Market B without any market-specific validation

analysis_questions: In practice, do borrowers deploy separate hooks instances per market?
Can a lender credentialed for a low-risk market access a high-risk market?
Is this documented as expected behavior?
validation_requirements: Deploy two markets with different access intents on same hooks instance
Grant credential for Market A only
Verify lender can deposit on Market B using Market A credential

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Borrower deploys hooks instance and creates Market A (public, low minimum deposit) and Market B (restricted, institutional). Lender gets credential via Market A's provider. The same credential allows deposit on Market B through shared _lenderStatus, bypassing Market B's intended restrictions.
prerequisites: Multiple markets on single hooks instance
Different access policies intended per market

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unauthorized access to restricted markets; regulatory compliance violation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All markets sharing the hooks instance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Borrower must deploy separate hooks instances per market

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for cross-market credential isolation

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain events would show deposit on unexpected market

threats.title: Reentrancy via validateCredential Provider Callback Can Corrupt Lender Status Storage
threats.hypothesis_description: _tryValidateCredential makes an external CALL (not staticcall) to a role provider's validateCredential function, passing all remaining gas. Since the lender's status is read into memory before this call and written to storage after it returns, a malicious provider can reenter the hooks contract during the callback to modify _lenderStatus in storage. When the original function resumes, _writeLenderStatus overwrites the storage with stale in-memory data, reverting any changes made during reentrancy. This enables a provider to corrupt credential state by having grantRole called during the callback and then having it overwritten.
threats.investigation_rationale: The call pattern is: read _lenderStatus to memory → external CALL to provider → modify memory status → write memory status to storage. This classic read-modify-write pattern with an external call in the middle is vulnerable to reentrancy if the external call can modify the same storage slot.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External CALL to provider between reading _lenderStatus to memory and writing it back to storage. Provider is a registered role provider that can call grantRole during callback.

preconditions: A registered role provider is a contract that reenters the hooks contract during validateCredential
The provider calls grantRole(victim, timestamp) during the callback
The original function's _writeLenderStatus overwrites the grantRole's storage write

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External call between storage read and storage write; no reentrancy guard
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Provider can call grantRole during validateCredential callback, but the in-memory status from before the callback overwrites the grant when _writeLenderStatus executes
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No reentrancy guard (nonReentrant modifier) on hooks functions

analysis_questions: Can a provider realistically reenter grantRole during the validateCredential callback?
Does the overwrite actually downgrade or corrupt the lender's credential?
Are there other functions the provider could reenter to cause harm?
validation_requirements: Deploy a malicious provider that calls grantRole during validateCredential
Verify that _writeLenderStatus overwrites the grantRole storage update
Check if the market contract has reentrancy guards that prevent this path

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Malicious provider is added by borrower. During onDeposit, lender provides hooksData pointing to malicious provider. Provider's validateCredential calls grantRole(lender, farFutureTimestamp) to give a long-lived credential, then returns a short-lived timestamp. The hooks contract first processes grantRole (writing long credential to storage), then _tryValidateCredential gets the short timestamp, and _writeLenderStatus overwrites with the short credential, silently downgrading the lender.
prerequisites: Borrower adds a malicious/compromised role provider
Lender uses that provider for credential validation

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Credential state corruption could lock lenders out of deposits or withdrawals
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lender must re-authenticate with a different provider

threats.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy tests for provider callbacks

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas forwarded to provider includes enough for reentrant grantRole call
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Multiple events emitted in single transaction would indicate reentrancy

threats.title: HooksConfig.onBorrow Uses Incorrect Constant RepayHook_Base_Size Instead of BorrowHook_Base_Size
threats.hypothesis_description: In HooksConfig.sol, the onBorrow function's assembly block uses RepayHook_Base_Size (0x0224) to calculate the calldata size instead of BorrowHook_Base_Size (0x0224). While both constants currently have the same value, this is a demonstrable code defect. If either constant is modified independently in a future update (e.g., adding parameters to onBorrow or onRepay), the onBorrow hook would send incorrectly sized calldata to the hooks contract, potentially truncating data or including garbage bytes.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly defines BorrowHook_Base_Size = 0x0224 as a separate constant from RepayHook_Base_Size = 0x0224, indicating they are intended to be independently maintained. Using the wrong constant is a maintenance hazard and a correctness defect.

proof_of_signal.lines: onBorrow assembly block, line with 'let size := add(RepayHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes)'
proof_of_signal.pattern: Uses RepayHook_Base_Size instead of BorrowHook_Base_Size; both equal 0x0224 currently but are defined as separate constants

preconditions: onBorrow hook is enabled for a market
Currently: no exploitable impact since values are equal
Future: values must diverge for exploitation

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Wrong constant name used for size calculation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: let size := add(RepayHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes) should be add(BorrowHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No compile-time check preventing use of wrong constant

analysis_questions: Do BorrowHook_Base_Size and RepayHook_Base_Size have the same value?
Could a future update cause them to diverge?
Is there any scenario where the calldata layout differs between borrow and repay?
validation_requirements: Confirm both constants equal 0x0224
Verify calldata structure for onBorrow matches RepayHook_Base_Size layout
Flag for correction regardless of current impact

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: No current exploitation path since constants are equal. If a future update changes one constant without the other, onBorrow would send incorrectly sized calldata, potentially causing the hooks contract to receive truncated or padded data, leading to incorrect hook behavior.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Currently zero; future risk of incorrect borrow hook behavior
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All markets with onBorrow hook enabled

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying correct constant usage in onBorrow assembly

threats.title: HooksConfig calldata-suffix handling may let any caller spoof provider-selection/credential-bytes (confused-deputy) across multiple market functions
threats.hypothesis_description: The HooksConfig library forwards the *entire trailing calldata suffix* ("extraData") from the market call to the hooks contract by computing extraCalldataBytes = calldatasize() - baseCalldataSize and calldatacopy(...) from baseCalldataSize. If the market does not strictly constrain calldata layout, an attacker may be able to append arbitrary bytes that the hooks interpret as (providerAddress, credentialData), selecting arbitrary providers and invoking validateCredential/getCredential in ways not intended by the market/borrower.
threats.investigation_rationale: Access control depends on hooksData semantics. If markets accept arbitrary trailing bytes (common if using assembly/low-level dispatch), attackers can pass crafted provider addresses/credential data to force hooks to call unexpected providers (stateful validateCredential) or to change lender status. This is especially relevant given _tryValidateCredential is stateful and can consume one-time credentials.
deployment_assumptions: The Market contract(s) that call HooksConfig.onDeposit/onTransfer/onQueueWithdrawal/etc. do not validate that no extra trailing bytes are present unless explicitly allowed
The market passes untrusted user calldata directly to HooksConfig.* without sanitizing/overriding the extraData region
threats.config_evidence: No Market contract code provided; HooksConfig explicitly copies "extraData from end of calldata" for multiple hooks (HooksConfig.sol).

proof_of_signal.lines: multiple locations (onDeposit/onQueueWithdrawal/onTransfer/...): assembly uses extraCalldataBytes := sub(calldatasize(), baseCalldataSize) and calldatacopy(..., baseCalldataSize, extraCalldataBytes)
proof_of_signal.pattern: untrusted trailing calldata is forwarded to hooks as extraData without validation/sanitization

preconditions: A market function is callable by untrusted users and uses HooksConfig.* to call hooks
Market does not enforce strict calldata size / ABI decoding that rejects extra trailing bytes
Hook implementation interprets extraData as provider selection / credential bytes (e.g., AccessControlHooks/FixedTermLoanHooks _handleHooksData)

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: HooksConfig.sol: all hook call builders copy calldata suffix as extraData
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: attacker-controlled providerAddress/data could be injected into hooksData parsing (first 20 bytes are provider address)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no explicit calldata-length equality checks in HooksConfig library (by design)

analysis_questions: Do market entrypoints reject calldata with extra trailing bytes (strict ABI decoding) or accept it?
Is there any path where a user can append (providerAddress, credentialData) and thus force validateCredential calls?
Do hook contracts rely on hooksData for critical authorization (deposit/transfer/withdrawal)?
validation_requirements: Inspect Market contract ABI decoding patterns; fuzz with extra trailing calldata bytes
End-to-end test: append 20+ bytes to a deposit/transfer call and confirm hooks see attacker-controlled hooksData
Confirm whether provider.validateCredential side effects can be triggered/abused

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls a market function (e.g., deposit/transfer) with crafted trailing bytes that the market ignores but HooksConfig forwards. Hooks then calls attacker-chosen provider.validateCredential, potentially granting access, consuming victim credentials, or causing provider side effects.
prerequisites: Ability to call a market function that uses HooksConfig forwarding
At least one approved role provider whose validateCredential has impactful side effects or permissive validation

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential access-control bypass (deposit/transfer/withdraw) or forced credential consumption; could lead to unauthorized token minting/transfer depending on market implementation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any lender interacting with affected markets; potentially system-wide if a shared hooks instance is used
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Depends on whether unauthorized deposits/withdrawals occur; credential side effects may be irreversible

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing tests for extra trailing calldata bytes on market entrypoints and their propagation into hooks

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain: crafted calldata visible; difficult to attribute intent unless provider emits events

threats.title: MarketConstraintHooks._calculateTemporaryReserveRatioBips can revert via division-by-zero if originalAnnualInterestBips == 0 in unexpected call paths
threats.hypothesis_description: The function computes relativeDiff = mulDiv(10000, originalAnnualInterestBips - annualInterestBips, originalAnnualInterestBips). If originalAnnualInterestBips is 0, this is a division-by-zero revert. The main hook tries to avoid calling it when originalAnnualInterestBips is 0, but edge states (e.g., tmp.expiry > 0 with originalAnnualInterestBips accidentally stored as 0, or inconsistent intermediateState passed by caller) could trigger this.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even if market logic 'shouldn't' set original APR to 0 during temp state, a single unexpected state can brick future APR updates (hook revert). This is meaningful because this hook is used as a constraint mechanism; bricking updates can be safety critical.
deployment_assumptions: A market can call onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips with intermediateState.originalAnnualInterestBips == 0 while still reaching the branch annualInterestBips < originalAnnualInterestBips, or tmp stored originals are 0
Caller-provided intermediateState may not be validated to match actual market state
threats.config_evidence: No Market contract code provided; MarketConstraintHooks trusts intermediateState values passed in (MarketConstraintHooks.sol onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips).

proof_of_signal.pattern: division by originalAnnualInterestBips without explicit zero guard in callee

preconditions: A call path reaches _calculateTemporaryReserveRatioBips with originalAnnualInterestBips == 0
Hook is enabled and called by market during APR update

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: APR update hook can revert, blocking parameter updates

analysis_questions: Can temporaryExcessReserveRatio[market].originalAnnualInterestBips ever be 0 while tmp.expiry > 0?
Does Market pass a trusted intermediateState reflecting real stored APR/reserveRatio?
Are there any state transitions that create temp state starting from APR=0?
validation_requirements: Symbolic execution / invariants: prove originalAnnualInterestBips > 0 when tmp.expiry > 0
If not provable, add guard or assert in helper/hook

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If an attacker can influence intermediateState (or if a buggy market passes 0 original APR), the hook reverts on APR changes, bricking interest updates.
prerequisites: Mis-specified market integration or corrupted temp state

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect but potentially significant (risk parameter updates bricked)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May require redeploying hooks/market or admin intervention

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for APR=0 and temp-state interactions

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detect reverts on APR update transactions

threats.title: onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips Temporary Reserve Ratio Can Be Manipulated Through Repeated APR Changes Within Active Period
threats.hypothesis_description: When a temporary excess reserve ratio is active and not expired, a borrower can further reduce the APR below the original. If the new APR is below originalAnnualInterestBips but above or equal to intermediateState.annualInterestBips, the expiry timer is NOT reset (line: expiry = tmp.expiry). However, the reserve ratio IS recalculated based on the new reduction from the original. This creates a scenario where the borrower can reduce APR in small steps during the same temporary period: the reserve ratio recalculates (potentially decreasing compared to a single large drop), while the original expiry is preserved. After expiry, the original reserve ratio is restored, not the intermediate elevated one.
threats.investigation_rationale: The timer preservation logic (annualInterestBips >= intermediateState.annualInterestBips keeps old expiry) combined with the reserve ratio recalculation creates an asymmetry. The borrower can partially raise APR to preserve the timer, then the expiry arrives sooner, allowing faster baseline resets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (annualInterestBips >= intermediateState.annualInterestBips) { expiry = tmp.expiry; } — timer preserved on partial recovery, combined with reserve ratio recalculation from original

preconditions: Active temporary reserve ratio period
Borrower makes intermediate APR changes within the 2-week window

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Expiry preserved when APR partially raised, but reserve ratio recalculated — creates timing manipulation opportunity
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Step 1: Drop APR sharply (triggers 2-week timer). Step 2: Partially raise APR (preserves old timer, recalculates lower reserve ratio). Step 3: Timer expires sooner than expected for the actual APR reduction.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Borrower achieves lower effective reserve requirement for a significant APR reduction by gaming the timer preservation logic

economic_questions: What is the maximum reserve ratio reduction achievable through this timing manipulation?
How does this interact with the 25% unpenalized threshold?
Can this be combined with the gradual step-down for amplified effect?
validation_requirements: Simulate multi-step APR changes within a single temporary period
Calculate effective reserve ratio at each step
Compare to the reserve ratio from a single equivalent APR drop

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Proportional to reserve ratio differential and market TVL

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During active temporary reserve ratio period

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced reserve protection below intended levels

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Timer preservation vs reserve ratio recalculation interaction

threats.title: Liquidity Requirement Underflow DoS via Double-Deduction of Pending Withdrawals
threats.hypothesis_description: `MarketState.liquidityRequired` calculates required reserves using `(state.scaledTotalSupply - scaledWithdrawals)`. According to protocol documentation, lenders 'burn market tokens' when queueing withdrawals. If burning tokens reduces `scaledTotalSupply` at the time of queueing, then `scaledTotalSupply` already represents only the active (non-withdrawing) supply. Subtracting `scaledWithdrawals` (pending withdrawals) a second time will cause an arithmetic underflow whenever pending withdrawals exceed the remaining active supply (i.e., >50% of original supply is queued). This underflow will revert `liquidityRequired`, permanently bricking all borrows, withdrawals, and state updates in the market.
threats.investigation_rationale: Mismatch between token burn timing and reserve calculation formula can lead to guaranteed underflows during mass withdrawal events, locking all market funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: scaledRequiredReserves = (state.scaledTotalSupply - scaledWithdrawals).bipMul(...)

preconditions: Market tokens are burned upon queueing a withdrawal
Pending withdrawals exceed remaining active supply

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes complete protocol insolvency and locked funds

economic_questions: Does queueing a withdrawal immediately reduce scaledTotalSupply?
Can a coordinated lender exit trigger this underflow?
validation_requirements: Verify Market contract accounting for queueWithdrawal
Test scenario where >50% of supply is queued for withdrawal

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Sufficient capital to hold >50% of market supply

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During market panic or coordinated exit

threats.title: Asymmetric Access Control Configuration Traps Secondary Market Buyers
threats.hypothesis_description: If a market is configured with `useOnTransfer = false` but `useOnQueueWithdrawal = true`, users can freely buy market tokens on secondary DEXs without a credential. However, because `transferRequiresAccess` is false, uncredentialed recipients are NOT marked as `isKnownLenderOnMarket`. When these buyers attempt to withdraw their underlying assets, `onQueueWithdrawal` will revert because they lack both a credential and known lender status. Their funds become permanently trapped in the market unless they can obtain a credential or sell at a loss.
threats.investigation_rationale: The interaction between transfer and withdrawal access flags creates a dead-end state for uncredentialed token recipients, breaking ERC20 composability.
deployment_assumptions: Market deployed with useOnTransfer=false and useOnQueueWithdrawal=true
threats.config_evidence: HooksConfig.sol allows independent setting of useOnTransfer and useOnQueueWithdrawal flags

proof_of_signal.pattern: canSetKnownLender requires hasValidCredential, leaving uncredentialed receivers unknown

preconditions: Market configured with asymmetric access flags
Secondary market exists for the market token

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: isKnownLenderOnMarket bypasses credential checks but is only set if credential exists
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Arbitrageurs buy tokens at discount but cannot redeem them, losing capital

economic_questions: Will arbitrageurs provide exit liquidity for trapped users?
Can the borrower exploit this to trap specific capital?
validation_requirements: Test transfer to uncredentialed user followed by withdrawal attempt
Verify config flag combinations allowed by factory

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Active DEX pool for market tokens

threats.title: Forced Hook Execution Causes O(N) Gas Griefing on Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: When a market requires withdrawal access, the hook contract forcibly enables the `Bit_Enabled_Transfer` flag. This causes the market to call `onTransfer` for every token transfer. Even if `transferRequiresAccess` is false, the hook still calls `_tryValidateAccessInner` for any recipient who is not a known lender. If the recipient lacks a credential, this triggers `_loopTryGetCredential`, making an external `staticcall` to EVERY registered pull provider. An attacker can exploit this by transferring 1 wei to many uncredentialed addresses, or the sheer baseline gas cost of multiple external calls could break DEX composability.
threats.investigation_rationale: Forced execution of credential validation loops on unrestricted transfers creates massive, unintended gas overhead.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _tryValidateAccessInner called even when transferRequiresAccess is false

preconditions: Market has withdrawal access required
Multiple pull providers registered

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: O(N) external calls per transfer where N is number of pull providers
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Network congestion griefing or breaking competitor DEX pools

economic_questions: What is the gas cost per transfer with 5-10 pull providers?
Does this exceed standard DEX router gas limits?
validation_requirements: Measure gas consumption of onTransfer with multiple pull providers
Test integration with Uniswap V2/V3 routers

threats.title: HooksConfig.onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips Checks returndatasize Before External Call Due to Yul Evaluation Order
threats.hypothesis_description: In HooksConfig.sol's `onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips`, the assembly block uses `if or(lt(returndatasize(), 0x40), iszero(call(gas(), target, ...)))`. Yul evaluates arguments left-to-right, so `returndatasize()` is read BEFORE the `call` is made. This means `lt(returndatasize(), 0x40)` checks the return data size from the PREVIOUS external call in the market's execution context, not from the hooks call. If no prior call returned >= 64 bytes (or no prior call was made), this evaluates to true, making the `or` always true, causing a revert even when the hooks call succeeds and returns valid data. This could brick the `setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips` functionality entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The developer's intent was to check both call failure AND insufficient return data. But Yul left-to-right evaluation means the returndatasize check uses stale data. Other hook functions (onDeposit, onTransfer, etc.) don't have this pattern since they return void. This is the only hook that expects return data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: or(lt(returndatasize(), 0x40), iszero(call(...))) evaluates returndatasize() before the call opcode executes; should be or(iszero(call(...)), lt(returndatasize(), 0x40)) or separate checks

preconditions: The market contract's execution path for setAnnualInterestBips must not include a prior external call returning >= 64 bytes before invoking this library function
The hooks flag useOnSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips must be enabled

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Yul evaluates `lt(returndatasize(), 0x40)` BEFORE the `call` opcode, checking stale RETURNDATASIZE from prior execution context
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Affects borrower's ability to adjust interest rates
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent if the market's execution path doesn't have a prior call with >= 64 bytes return data

operational_questions: What is the exact execution path in the market contract before calling this library function?
Does the market contract make any external calls returning >= 64 bytes before this point?
Can this be triggered deterministically to permanently brick interest rate changes?

Call setAnnualInterestBips and check if it reverts with the hooks return data

threats.title: Gas Griefing via Malicious Pull Provider Consuming All Gas in Credential Loop
threats.hypothesis_description: Both _tryGetCredential and _loopTryGetCredential pass all remaining gas to external pull providers via `staticcall(gas(), providerAddress, ...)`. A malicious or buggy pull provider can consume all available gas (e.g., infinite loop), causing the entire credential validation to revert with Out of Gas. Since _loopTryGetCredential iterates over all pull providers, even a single malicious provider can DoS all credential validation, preventing deposits, transfers, and withdrawals for all lenders on all markets using the hooks instance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The gas() opcode forwards all remaining gas to the staticcall. There is no gas limit cap for the external call. A single malicious provider in the _pullProviders array blocks all credential refresh attempts since the loop encounters the OOG revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: staticcall(gas(), providerAddress, ...) forwards all remaining gas; no gas cap; failure propagates as OOG to parent

preconditions: A pull provider must be registered that consumes excessive gas (malicious or buggy)
The lender's credential validation must reach the loop path (no valid cached credential)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: All gas forwarded to external calls with no cap; single provider failure DoSes entire loop
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Borrower must have added the malicious provider (or provider was later compromised)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent until borrower removes the malicious provider

operational_questions: Does the staticcall's failure propagate as an OOG or as a silent false return?
Can the borrower remove the malicious provider quickly enough to restore service?
Is there a gas limit that would prevent the loop from being fully DoSed?
validation_requirements: Deploy a gas-consuming mock pull provider
Register it and verify credential validation fails with OOG

threats.title: Borrow hook-call assembly in HooksConfig may extract extraCalldataBytes from wrong offset, enabling oversized hook calldata and gas griefing in markets that use onBorrow
threats.hypothesis_description: HooksConfig.onBorrow computes `extraCalldataBytes := sub(calldatasize(), BorrowCalldataSize)` and then copies that many bytes from calldata starting at BorrowCalldataSize (0x24). For borrow calls whose real base calldata size is not exactly 0x24 (e.g., if the market's borrow signature includes more args or uses different encoding), this library may miscompute extraCalldataBytes, causing large calldatacopy and inflated hook calldata, potentially reverting due to OOG or calling hooks with attacker-controlled trailing bytes.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical integration hazard: HooksConfig is a low-level library that assumes specific calldata layouts. If the Market contract’s borrow function signature/encoding differs or evolves, users can cause borrow transactions to fail or become expensive. Phase D should confirm the exact Market function signatures and how baseCalldataSize is passed (note: other hook builders accept baseCalldataSize; onBorrow does not).
deployment_assumptions: A deployed Market uses HooksConfig.onBorrow and allows user-supplied extra calldata (suffix) beyond the expected base
Market.borrow calldata base size differs from 0x24 in production (or can be made to differ by optional params)
Hook is enabled for onBorrow (useOnBorrow == true)
threats.config_evidence: HooksConfig.onBorrow hardcodes BorrowCalldataSize=0x24 and does not accept baseCalldataSize parameter, unlike onQueueWithdrawal/onTransfer/onRepay.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Hardcoded `BorrowCalldataSize = 0x24` and `extraCalldataBytes := sub(calldatasize(), BorrowCalldataSize)` without a `baseCalldataSize` argument; calldatacopy uses that derived length

preconditions: Market uses this library’s onBorrow hook path
useOnBorrow flag is enabled in the market HooksConfig
Attacker can submit a borrow call with crafted calldata length or with additional trailing bytes that the market does not reject

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hook builder for borrow differs from other hook builders by not taking baseCalldataSize, increasing risk of mis-sizing and copying unexpected data.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires onBorrow enabled in config (often optional).

threats.title: RoleProvider.setNotPullProvider bitmask may corrupt fields, causing misclassification and index inconsistency (credential refresh bypass/DoS)
threats.hypothesis_description: LibRoleProvider.setNotPullProvider uses a raw OR mask (0xffffff0000000000) to set pullProviderIndex to NotPullProviderIndex, but the mask appears too small and positioned ambiguously relative to the packed layout (TTL at bits 224..255, address at bits 64..223, pullProviderIndex at bits 40..63). If the mask is misaligned, it can accidentally set bits inside the providerAddress region rather than only the pullProviderIndex field. This could corrupt providerAddress or TTL, leading to incorrect provider lookups, providers becoming unreachable, or a provider being treated as pull/push incorrectly, breaking access validation and potentially locking users out.
threats.investigation_rationale: Packing/unpacking is heavily assembly-based; a single-bitfield bug can cascade into access control failures across all markets. The suspicious mask is not derived from the same masks used in setPullProviderIndex, increasing risk of mismatch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: hard-coded OR mask to set pullProviderIndex; may overlap other packed fields

preconditions: setNotPullProvider is used anywhere in production code paths (directly or indirectly) OR similar bit patterns are assumed elsewhere
A corrupted RoleProvider entry is stored into _roleProviders / _pullProviders and later read for access validation
Markets depend on pull provider classification for refresh loops

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: manual bit-twiddling without field-specific AND-mask prior to OR
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: mask likely does not target exactly bits 40..63; could flip address bits
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no unit tests/assertions verifying encode/decode invariants after setNotPullProvider

analysis_questions: Is setNotPullProvider called anywhere (grep usage) or planned for use in future versions?
Does 0xffffff0000000000 correctly set bits 40..63 to 1 without touching other fields?
Do encode/decode roundtrip tests pass after applying setNotPullProvider on random RoleProvider values?

threats.title: Secondary Role Provider Can Overwrite and Revoke Primary Credentials
threats.hypothesis_description: A secondary role provider can overwrite a user's credential by granting one with a slightly longer expiry, becoming the `lastProvider`, and then immediately revoking it to completely strip the user's access.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `grantRole` function allows any approved provider to replace an existing credential if the new expiry is greater than the old one. Once replaced, the new provider becomes the `lastProvider` and gains the exclusive right to call `revokeRole`. A malicious secondary provider can abuse this to selectively DoS users.

threats.title: MarketState.withdrawableProtocolFees Underflow Causes DoS on Protocol Fee Collection
threats.hypothesis_description: MarketState.withdrawableProtocolFees computes `totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals` without safe subtraction. In Solidity 0.8.x, this underflows and reverts when totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals, which can occur during severe undercollateralization or bad debt scenarios. This prevents protocol fee collection, potentially causing cascading failures in fee-dependent operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function uses raw subtraction `totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals` (line 95) without using satSub or similar safe subtraction. Unlike borrowableAssets which uses satSub for the same type of calculation, this function will revert. If protocol fee withdrawal is called during undercollateralization, the revert could block critical protocol operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `uint256 totalAvailableAssets = totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals;` uses raw subtraction. Compare with borrowableAssets (line 100) which uses `totalAssets.satSub(state.liquidityRequired())`.

preconditions: Market becomes undercollateralized (totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals)
Protocol attempts to collect accrued fees during this state

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not direct profit but blocks protocol fee collection; could prevent market state updates if fee collection is part of a critical path

threats.title: FixedTermLoanHooks Allows Zero-Duration Fixed Term at Market Creation
threats.hypothesis_description: FixedTermLoanHooks._onCreateMarket validates fixedTermEndTime with `fixedTermEndTime < block.timestamp` (strict less-than). Setting fixedTermEndTime = block.timestamp passes validation, creating a market where the term instantly expires. Lenders who deposit expecting a meaningful fixed-term guarantee have no actual withdrawal restriction since `fixedTermEndTime > block.timestamp` is immediately false.
threats.investigation_rationale: The onQueueWithdrawal check is `if (market.fixedTermEndTime > block.timestamp) revert WithdrawBeforeTermEnd()`. With fixedTermEndTime = block.timestamp in the creation block, in any subsequent block this check passes, allowing immediate withdrawal queueing. The FixedTermLoanHooks product name implies a meaningful lock period, but the code allows a zero-effective-term market.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation uses `fixedTermEndTime < block.timestamp` (strict less-than), allowing fixedTermEndTime == block.timestamp. onQueueWithdrawal (line 440) uses `fixedTermEndTime > block.timestamp`, which is false when equal.

preconditions: Borrower calls factory with hooksData encoding fixedTermEndTime = block.timestamp
Lenders deposit expecting a fixed-term loan product

threats.title: grantRoles Unbounded Loop Enables Gas Griefing by Malicious Role Provider
threats.hypothesis_description: The grantRoles function in both AccessControlHooks and FixedTermLoanHooks accepts unbounded arrays of accounts and timestamps. Any approved role provider can call this function with arbitrarily large arrays. Each iteration performs storage reads and writes (_lenderStatus, _roleProviders). A malicious role provider could call grantRoles with thousands of entries, potentially exceeding the block gas limit or consuming excessive gas, delaying other transactions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function has no array size limit. While the caller pays for gas, a malicious provider approved by the borrower could use this to exhaust gas in a way that blocks other operations if called within a multi-step transaction or callback context.
deployment_assumptions: Borrower has approved a role provider that calls grantRoles with large arrays
threats.config_evidence: No array size limit in grantRoles. Role providers can call with any array length.

proof_of_signal.pattern: grantRoles has unbounded for loop: `for (uint256 i = 0; i < accounts.length; i++) { _grantRole(...) }` with no maximum array size check.

preconditions: Caller is an approved role provider
Provider calls grantRoles with a very large array

threats.title: Configuration mismatch: FixedTermLoanHooks always enables QueueWithdrawal hook but can set withdrawalRequiresAccess=false, enabling unrestricted post-term withdrawal queueing
threats.hypothesis_description: FixedTermLoanHooks marks QueueWithdrawal as a required hook via its immutable config, but withdrawalRequiresAccess is derived from parameters.hooks.useOnQueueWithdrawal() BEFORE mergeFlags(config). If the market deploy parameters set useOnQueueWithdrawal=false, the merged hooks config will still cause the market to call onQueueWithdrawal, but FixedTermLoanHooks will treat withdrawalRequiresAccess=false and therefore will not enforce credential validation (it only enforces fixed-term timing). After fixedTermEndTime passes, any holder of market tokens could queue withdrawals without being credentialed.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical integration defect: two sources of truth (merged hook flags vs the HookedMarket.withdrawalRequiresAccess snapshot) can diverge. That divergence can weaken access control in production without obvious signs, depending on deployment parameters.
deployment_assumptions: A market is deployed with FixedTermLoanHooks and parameters.hooks.useOnQueueWithdrawal() == false
The merged config forces onQueueWithdrawal hook enabled (required flag), so hook is invoked despite snapshot being false
threats.config_evidence: FixedTermLoanHooks._onCreateMarket sets `withdrawalRequiresAccess: marketHooksConfig.useOnQueueWithdrawal()` before `marketHooksConfig = marketHooksConfig.mergeFlags(config)` and config.requiredFlags includes QueueWithdrawal.

proof_of_signal.lines: _onCreateMarket: sets HookedMarket.withdrawalRequiresAccess from pre-merge flags; then mergeFlags(config) which requires QueueWithdrawal
proof_of_signal.pattern: withdrawalRequiresAccess derived from `marketHooksConfig.useOnQueueWithdrawal()` before `mergeFlags(config)`

preconditions: Market deployer supplies hooks config that disables onQueueWithdrawal in parameters
Market uses the merged hooks config to call onQueueWithdrawal anyway (expected)
Attacker holds market tokens (acquired via transfer on a market where transferRequiresAccess is false, or from a credentialed sender if transfer requires access)

threats.title: HooksConfig.mergeFlags/encodeHooksDeploymentConfig bitfield slicing mistakes could silently enable/disable hooks, weakening access control or constraints
threats.hypothesis_description: HooksConfig/HooksDeploymentConfig use nontrivial assembly slicing/shifting to merge optional and required flags. If any bit offset assumptions are wrong (e.g., flags not aligned to expected 16-bit window, incorrect shifts of 0x50/0x40), the merged hooks config used by markets may not match intended security posture. This can lead to hooks not being called (bypass constraints) or being called unexpectedly (operational DoS). The risk is increased by maintenance hazards (many constants, manual offsets, and multiple call constructors).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a systemic configuration integrity risk: if hook enabling is wrong, all other hook logic is moot. Attackers exploit the resulting bypass by using market functions that should be hooked but are not.
deployment_assumptions: Market contract relies solely on HooksConfig flags to decide which hooks to call
Deployment populates HooksConfig values that are merged using these library functions (i.e., uses mergeFlags/config pattern)
threats.config_evidence: No Market contract code provided; need to verify it uses HooksConfig.mergeFlags output and respects the same bit layout.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Manual bit slicing: `shr(0x50, optionalFlags)`, `shr(0x40, requiredFlags)`, masks `0xffff`/`0xffff0000`

preconditions: There exists a mismatch between intended enabled hooks and actual merged bits
A market action depends on a hook being called for security (deposit/transfer/queueWithdrawal/parameter constraints)

threats.title: Borrower Can Set fixedTermEndTime to Zero Eliminating Withdrawal Lock for Fixed-Term Markets
threats.hypothesis_description: FixedTermLoanHooks.setFixedTermEndTime only checks that newFixedTermEndTime <= hookedMarket.fixedTermEndTime (cannot increase). Setting newFixedTermEndTime to 0 passes this check for any existing value. Since onQueueWithdrawal checks `if (market.fixedTermEndTime > block.timestamp)` and 0 is never > block.timestamp, setting the term to 0 immediately allows all lenders to queue withdrawals. The borrower could use this after reducing APR to release lenders from a now-unfavorable position, or combine with other tactics.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the monotonic decrease of fixedTermEndTime is by design, allowing it to go to 0 (effectively disabling the fixed term entirely) may not be the intended behavior. A minimum remaining term or a floor above 0 would prevent this.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only check is newFixedTermEndTime > hookedMarket.fixedTermEndTime → revert. Setting to 0 always passes since 0 <= any uint32. No minimum floor or non-zero requirement.

Market was created with a non-zero fixedTermEndTime

threats.title: LibStoredInitCode.create2WithStoredInitCode* uses free memory without updating free-memory pointer, enabling memory clobbering in composed deployment scripts
threats.hypothesis_description: LibStoredInitCode reads `mload(0x40)` into initCodePointer and writes initcode/constructor args there via extcodecopy/mcopy, but never updates the free memory pointer (`mstore(0x40, ...)`). In complex scripts or onchain contracts that compose these functions with other memory allocations, subsequent Solidity code may overwrite the initcode buffer, causing deployment of corrupted bytecode or unexpected reverts.
threats.investigation_rationale: While primarily a deployment utility, this is still a code defect that can lead to deploying wrong code, which is catastrophic if used in production factories/scripts. It is a realistic source of misdeployment, especially when multiple deployments occur in a single transaction/script and memory is heavily used.
deployment_assumptions: Project uses LibStoredInitCode in production deployment flows beyond simple isolated calls
Deployment scripts/contracts perform additional memory allocations after calling create2WithStoredInitCode* but before the CREATE2 executes (or reuse the same pointer incorrectly)
threats.config_evidence: LibStoredInitCode is imported and used by LibDeployment.broadcastDeployInitcode (LibDeployment.sol). No evidence of onchain factory usage provided; risk depends on how these libraries are composed.

proof_of_signal.lines: create*/create2*: `let initCodePointer := mload(0x40)` then writes to it; no `mstore(0x40, ...)` after
proof_of_signal.pattern: free-memory pointer not advanced after writing dynamic buffer

preconditions: Library functions used in a context where subsequent Solidity code assumes mload(0x40) still points to free memory
Memory is allocated/written after the initcode buffer is prepared and before create/create2 reads it

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: writes to free memory without bumping free memory pointer
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: deployment of corrupted initcode or constructor args due to memory reuse

analysis_questions: Are these functions ever used inside onchain factories (not only forge scripts)?
Do deployment scripts perform additional operations after preparing initcode buffer that might clobber memory?
Is there any known pattern relying on this buffer persisting across calls?
validation_requirements: Write a regression test that calls create2WithStoredInitCode with constructorArgs, then allocates memory before CREATE2 and verify corruption
Fix by updating free memory pointer: mstore(0x40, initCodePointer + alignedSize)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A deployment script/factory composes multiple library calls; memory clobber results in deploying wrong bytecode at deterministic addresses, potentially leading to loss of control over deployments or bricked system wiring.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potentially severe if wrong contracts are deployed and wired; could lead to irreversible misconfiguration

threats.title: Deployment utility LibStoredInitCode.create2WithStoredInitCode* does not advance free memory pointer, risking memory clobber and incorrect initcode/constructor args in complex scripts
threats.hypothesis_description: LibStoredInitCode functions allocate initcode at `mload(0x40)` but do not update the free memory pointer after copying extcode and appending constructor args. In complex Foundry scripts/libraries that make subsequent memory allocations after calling these functions (or reenter into Solidity code that relies on the free memory pointer), this can clobber the initcode buffer or other memory, leading to wrong deployments (unexpected constructor args, wrong bytecode, deployment failure). While this is primarily an ops/deployment risk, it can manifest as production misdeployment of hooks/markets or initcode storage contracts.
threats.investigation_rationale: Misdeployment is a common root cause for operational incidents. This library is used by LibDeployment for initcode storage deployments, and incorrect deployment artifacts/addresses can cascade into hooking wrong contracts or registering wrong providers.
deployment_assumptions: Project uses LibStoredInitCode in deployment scripts with additional memory allocations after the call in the same execution context
Deployed bytecode/constructor args integrity is security-relevant (e.g., wrong hooks address wired into markets)
threats.config_evidence: LibDeployment.broadcastDeployInitcode() uses LibStoredInitCode.deployInitCode(), and deployments rely on scripts; no direct on-chain runtime usage.

proof_of_signal.lines: create2WithStoredInitCode* assembly: `let initCodePointer := mload(0x40)` then extcodecopy/mcopy; no `mstore(0x40, ...)`
proof_of_signal.pattern: Uses free memory pointer as buffer but never updates it

preconditions: Deployment script calls LibStoredInitCode.create2WithStoredInitCode* and then performs other operations that allocate memory before CREATE2 executes (or within same assembly block assumptions)
Misdeployment would not be immediately detected before wiring contracts together

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Memory-unsafe assembly in deployment utilities can cause silent misdeployment.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Ops/deployment pipeline behavior and script complexity.

operational_questions: Are these functions used in production deployments (not only tests)?
Do deployment scripts allocate additional memory after calling these functions within the same call stack?
Are deployed addresses verified on-chain against expected bytecode hashes/constructor args?
validation_requirements: Run deployment scripts with instrumentation to detect memory pointer reuse/clobber
Add post-deploy bytecode verification and constructor arg checks
Unit test deterministic CREATE2 deployments with varying constructor args sizes

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Induce/observe a complex deployment script path using create2WithStoredInitCode* repeatedly.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Memory clobber causes wrong initcode or args to be used for a deployment.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Market/hook wiring uses wrong addresses, potentially disabling access control or constraints.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires deployment pipeline weakness; not an on-chain attacker action.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Indirect: miswired hooks/providers can create security gaps.
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium: faulty deployments, broken markets, incorrect artifacts.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High: non-upgradable system may require redeployment and migration.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Deployed bytecode not matching expected artifacts; inconsistent constructor args files vs on-chain runtime behavior.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Intermittent deployment failures or nondeterministic CREATE2 results.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Only manifests in complex deployment sequences or large constructor args.
